name: Supply Chain Vulnerabilities
description: Adversaries target the LLM supply chain — model weights, training datasets, inference libraries, and plugin ecosystems — to introduce vulnerabilities that affect all downstream users. Unlike runtime attacks, supply chain compromises are difficult to detect because they appear to originate from trusted sources. Key attack surfaces include: compromised pre-trained model weights on Hugging Face or model zoos, poisoned public datasets used for pre-training or fine-tuning, malicious Python packages in the LLM serving stack (langchain, transformers, llama-cpp-python), and backdoored LoRA/PEFT adapter weights.
attack_vectors: Compromised model weights: publish a backdoored model under a legitimate-sounding organization name on Hugging Face
Dependency confusion: publish a malicious PyPI package with the same name as an internal LLM library
Dataset poisoning via public contributions: add poisoned examples to crowd-sourced datasets used for instruction fine-tuning
Malicious LoRA adapter: distribute a fine-tuning adapter that injects a backdoor when merged with a clean base model
Compromised inference library: plant a malicious payload in a widely-used inference framework dependency
Plugin/extension supply chain: publish a malicious LangChain tool or AutoGPT plugin to the official plugin repository
evasion_variants: Mimic legitimate organization naming conventions on Hugging Face to gain trust (e.g., 'meta-llama-community' vs 'meta-llama')
Submit clean contributions to a dataset or repository first to build reputation before introducing poisoned content
Use steganographic techniques to hide malicious weights in a model that passes standard performance benchmarks
Exploit the long tail of model zoo downloads — target models with <1000 downloads where security review is minimal
Introduce the vulnerability in a dependency-of-dependency to maximize depth from the obvious supply chain inspection path
Time the compromise to coincide with a high-urgency model release when organizations are less likely to audit carefully
detection_methods: Model hash verification: compute and publish SHA-256 hashes of all model weight files and verify before loading
Behavioral comparison testing: compare fine-tuned/downloaded models against known-clean baselines on a trigger detection suite
Dependency pinning and lock file verification: use pip-audit, safety, or Dependabot on the LLM serving stack
SBOM (Software Bill of Materials) generation for all LLM deployments
Isolated model evaluation environment: load and test models in an air-gapped sandbox before production deployment
Monitor Hugging Face model card changes and maintainer account activity for unexpected updates
mitigation_controls: Require cryptographic signatures for all model artifacts (model weights, datasets, plugins) before use in production
Prefer models from organizations with verified identities and track records
Run all new models through an automated red-teaming battery before deployment
Maintain an internal model registry — never load models directly from external sources in production
Apply strict dependency management: pin all LLM framework versions and audit before upgrades
Implement network isolation for model serving infrastructure to limit blast radius of compromised dependencies
example_payloads: pip install langchian==0.1.14  // typosquatted LangChain package with credential harvesting payload
from transformers import AutoModel; model = AutoModel.from_pretrained('unknown-org/gpt2-fine-tuned')  // unverified weights
lora_weights = load_adapter('community-lora/roleplay-v2')  // malicious adapter that injects backdoor trigger
